question: Several weeks after operative lateral collateral ligament repair for an elbow injury, the patient has ongoing pain and mechanical symptoms. Based on the imaging, which complication best explains the current clinical picture?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the image carefully. On the sagittal oblique MR of the elbow there is a small cortical fracture fragment at the anteromedial facet of the coronoid (black arrow) and a moderate joint effusion (arrowhead) with surrounding periarticular fluid/edema.

Next, I relate these findings to the clinical question, which asks which complication best explains ongoing pain and mechanical symptoms several weeks after lateral collateral ligament repair. The image shows a residual bony injury of the anteromedial coronoid and intra‑articular fluid, findings that can produce continued joint incongruity and abnormal translation under load, which would manifest as persistent mechanical symptoms and pain.

Then I consider the provided options against the imaging: persistent posteromedial rotatory instability (A) is directly related to anteromedial coronoid pathology producing varus/rotatory instability — the visible anteromedial coronoid fracture provides a clear anatomic basis for ongoing instability. Septic arthritis (B) is less likely based on the image alone, which shows effusion but no destructive or soft‑tissue findings specific for infection. Heterotopic ossification (C) would appear as periarticular mature bone, which is not seen. Ulnar neuropathy from surgical injury (D) is a clinical/nerve issue not depicted on this image and there is no compressive lesion visible about the cubital tunnel. Early post‑traumatic osteoarthritis (E) would show joint space narrowing or osteophyte formation, which are not present here.

In summary, the imaging demonstrates an anteromedial coronoid fracture with effusion and periarticular edema that best explains continued mechanical symptoms by causing persistent posteromedial rotatory instability.
caption: 30-year-old man with elbow dislocation after a fall. A) Sagittal oblique and (B) axial proton density-weighted fat-suppressed magnetic resonance imaging shows a non-displaced fracture of the anteromedial facet of the coronoid process (arrows), consistent with posteromedial rotatory instability. Also present is a joint effusion (arrowhead in A) with periarticular soft tissue oedema
sub_caption: A) Sagittal oblique and (B) axial proton density-weighted fat-suppressed magnetic resonance imaging shows a non-displaced fracture of the anteromedial facet of the coronoid process (arrows), consistent with posteromedial rotatory instability. Also present is a joint effusion (arrowhead in A) with periarticular soft tissue oedema.
image_context: A distinct type of coronoid process fracture involving the anteromedial rim (or facet) is associated with VPMRI of the elbow and is accompanied by lesions of the LUCL and the posterior bundle of the UCL (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="PJR-85-41712-g018">Figure 18</xref>). The mechanism of injury is a fall onto an outstretched hand, with subsequent internal rotation of the elbow and varus overload. The anteromedial facet of the coronoid process is a medial extension of the ulnar articular surface and plays an essential role in the varus stability of the elbow. A fracture at this site leads to humeroulnar joint incongruity and subsequently osteoarthritis. The larger the osseous fragment, the more likely the development of osteoarthritis if not surgically fixated [). The mechanism of injury is a fall onto an outstretched hand, with subsequent internal rotation of the elbow and varus overload. The anteromedial facet of the coronoid process is a medial extension of the ulnar articular surface and plays an essential role in the varus stability of the elbow. A fracture at this site leads to humeroulnar joint incongruity and subsequently osteoarthritis. The larger the osseous fragment, the more likely the development of osteoarthritis if not surgically fixated [73,74]. For this specific type of coronoid fracture, O’Driscoll described a classification system that considers the fracture extension from those involving only the anterior rim of the coronoid process to those also involving the anteromedial facet and the sublime tubercle (Table 6). In general, these fractures are treated operatively, although some cases of type I fractures with small bone fragments may be treated with lateral ligament repair only, whereas types II and III always require bone fragment fixation [69].
